---
title: "Why AI Didn’t Stop Me From Learning to Code"
description: An affirmation based on optimism and caution.
date: 2025-08-15
categories: [Machine Learning, Artificial Intelligence, Learn to Code, Bioinformatics]
draft: true
image: woof.png
comments:
  giscus:
    repo: ammaraldawood/ammaraldawood.github.io
date-modified: last-modified
citation: 
  url: https://ammaraldawood.com/blog/posts/2025-08-15

format:
    html:
        css: styles.css
---

It was June of 2022 when I first decided to delve (back) into mathematics and computation.
This was induced by the fact that biology and medicine have been turning into computational fields of
sorts due to the advent of high throughput sequencing and advanced simulation capabilities.
Biology provides a wealth of high-dimensional information unlike any other of the natural sciences,
medicine benefits from having room to make trials and errors before applying on humans,
and statistical methods applied through computation and code are shaping up to be the future of the 
those fields, and as a medical laboratory scientist whose research will mostly involve large amounts
of data that needs to be analyzed in all sorts of ways, I was left with very little room to keep dodging 
this undertaking.

I began my learning journey with the programming language Swift, which is mainly used
to develop for Apple platforms. I thought that Swift Playgrounds looked like a great interactive tool
that would make programming concepts stick. Not too long after, I signed up for Angela Yu's Udemy course
to learn Swift app development.

# A dilemma

A couple of months later, in November of 2022, the service ChatGPT, which was based on the GPT-3.5 model,
got launched and managed to become the fastest-growing consumer application in history, with nearly all of
the headlines
